content.full_text:
August 18, 1992
AUE IJ
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
California Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 958801-0807
}<C PM
NORTH VAI-LEY -\lt2
NBC
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
KCPM-TV, the NBC affiliate serving Chico, Redding and California's
North Valley, in order to counter criticism now being leveled against the broadcast
media, nation-wide, (i.e. the 3Gsecond sound bites) has extended an offer of one hour
"free" broadcast time to candidates in certain political races that are of great concern
and interest to the voters of the North Valley. This air time is set aside to be used as a
"I-eague of Women Voters" type format debate with KCPM-TV's News Director and
Managing Editors of North Valley newspapers asking the questions.
KCPM-TV does not have a policy of providing "free" air time (advertising
time or debate time) to either candidates or ballot issues.
KCPM-TV hereby makes the following request for informal advice.
Certain candidates have accepted our offer, unqualified. Other candidates
fail to respond to our letters, acknowledge our letters, nor do they return our telephone
calls. KCPM-TV in an effort to better inform our viewers on the issues facing the North
Valley, the state of Califomia and America desires to proceed with the debates, but
failing to get a positive response or acknowledgement from one candidate, in the event
we proceed with the appearance and questioning of the other candidate, would the
doliar valuc cf the air time uscC for this qucstion ar-rd aaswer sessron '#th oniy cne
candidate be considered a corporate contribution?
We acknowledge that informal assistance does not provide KCPM-TV with
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice, as set out in Government
Code Section 83114. We do respectfully request your informal advice at your earliest
convenience in order that we may either proceed with the scheduling and promotion of
these programs and confirm the dates with the accepting candidates; or, notifo the
candidates of KCPM-TV's inability to grant such free air time.
Respectfully submitted,
$%\irr\u,,0^
B. E. Goldfarb\ \ 
\
General Manager
180 E. 4th St. . Chico, CA 95928. (9t61 893-2424
2148 Redcliff Drive o Redding, CA 96002 . (916) 221-2424

sections.question: Certain candidates have accepted our offer, unqualified. Other candidates fail to respond to our letters, acknowledge our letters, nor do they return our telephone calls. KCPM-TV in an effort to better inform our viewers on the issues facing the North Valley, the state of California and America desires to proceed with the debates, but failing to get a positive response or acknowledgement from one candidate, in the event we proceed with the appearance and questioning of the other candidate, would the dollar value of the air time used for this question and answer session with only one candidate be considered a corporate contribution?
sections.question_synthetic: Would providing free broadcast time for a debate with only one candidate (due to another candidate's non-response) constitute a corporate contribution under FPPC regulations?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from KCPM-TV to the FPPC requesting informal advice. No FPPC response or conclusion sections are present. Document clearly identifies itself as requesting informal advice under Government Code Section 83114. Minor OCR artifacts noted in signature area.

KCPM-TV in an effort to better inform our viewers on the issues facing the North Valley, the state of California and America desires to proceed with the debates, but failing to get a positive response or acknowledgement from one candidate, in the event we proceed with the appearance and questioning of the other candidate, would the dollar value of the air time used for this question and answer session with only one candidate be considered a corporate contribution?

embedding.summary: KCPM-TV, an NBC affiliate, requests informal FPPC advice on whether offering free broadcast time for a debate format involving only one candidate (due to other candidates' non-responsiveness) would constitute a prohibited corporate contribution.
